text:
Artificial Intelligence (AI) slowly evolved from a concept into an essential part of our daily lives. We are going through eras of technology so swiftly that we don’t notice our shifting dependencies. Yet, if we take one little pause, we can see that this transformation has been unfolding over the past 70 years. We’ve transitioned from the early days of the World Wide Web to the rise of blockchain technology, from flip phones to smartphones, and now, AI has woven itself into the fabric of nearly every industry. In the creative field, the role of AI in architecture has become a topic of intense debate, reshaping the way we design and build our environment.
At its essence, AI is the science of creating machines that can think, learn, and make decisions, much like humans do. This journey began in the early 1950s with pioneers like Alan Turing, posing the question: Can machines think? In 1956, this hypothetical turned into a field of study at the Dartmouth Convention with John McCarthy coining the term “artificial intelligence” and arguing whether machines are capable of emulating complex human behaviors such as moral reasoning. Since that revolutionary moment in time, AI has evolved dramatically, driven by advancements in machine learning, neural networks, natural language processing, computer vision, and robotics.
From my perspective, AI advancements are not just about efficiency. They’re about redefining what’s possible. AI is no longer just a tool; it’s becoming an integral part of how we solve problems and innovate in fields as diverse as art, medicine, business, science, and, intrinsically, architecture. This article will explore the early integrations, current applications, challenges, ethics, and future of AI in architecture to hopefully shed light on this new era we’re marching into.
The Integration of AI into Architecture
The 1960s marked a revolutionary milestone in the field of architecture, as CAD software removed the architect from the drafting table to a computer-mounted desk. Centuries of architectural practice hinged on that moment in time when architects ceased to create with their hands. The advancement in CAD laid the groundwork for the forthcoming integration of AI applications by digitizing the design process.
Since then, designers and architects recognized the potential of computer technologies to translate complex forms into constructible designs. This ultimately paved the way for parametric design in the 1980s and the rise of BIM software. Particularly, in 1982 with Graphisoft’s ArchiCAD, a software that enabled object-oriented design, where changes to one part of the model automatically updates all related elements.
The earliest AI-driven design advancements can be traced back to Professor Patrick Jensen’s 2004 research at the University of Hong Kong, where he explored neural networks to optimize space layouts based on criteria like accessibility, functionality, and aesthetics. While initially theoretical, his work laid the groundwork for practical AI applications in design.
In the mid-2010s, the Autodesk Dreamcatcher project emerged as an early AI-driven generative design tool, automatically generating design options based on criteria like material efficiency, structural integrity, and environmental impact. It marked a key milestone in AI’s integration into architecture, offering designers customizable, optimized solutions.
AI Applications in Architecture
1. Generative Design
AI-driven generative design is pushing the boundaries of creativity by using algorithms to explore a vast array of design possibilities. The approach of automatically generating alternatives based on criteria such as materials, cost, and spatial requirements shifts the traditional design process and allows architects to focus on defining the problem to find quicker solutions.
Autodesk demonstrated the creative potential of their generative AI tool by using it to design their Toronto office. The AI, incorporating employee preferences and spatial constraints, generated layouts that perfectly balanced functionality with comfort.
2. Urban Planning
Through the analysis of vast datasets – traffic patterns, population density, and environmental factors – to optimize city layouts, AI is transforming urban planning. The swift simulation of various scenarios helps AI identify the most efficient and sustainable solutions for urban development, as it can optimize energy use, reduce carbon footprint, and improve public transportation.
Cities all around the world are utilizing AI to create resilient cities. Notable projects like Sidewalk Labs in Toronto and Singapore’s Smart Nation Initiative showcase AI’s role in creating resilient, smart cities that efficiently manage resources and adapt to future challenges like climate change.
Read more on Applications of parametric design in urban planning.
3. Predictive Maintenance
Predictive maintenance utilizes AI algorithms to analyze sensor data from systems like HVAC, lighting, and plumbing to forecast potential failures. This proactive approach allows for timely repairs and extends the lifespan of building components.
For instance, Siemens’ Navigator platform and Honeywell’s Forge use AI-driven insights to monitor performance and optimize efficiency. These technologies ensure that buildings operate at peak performance while maintaining sustainability, reducing unexpected costs, and energy waste.
4. Sustainable Design
The use of AI in architecture shines in the sustainable design scene, as it perfectly optimizes building designs with accurate data for energy efficiency, material use, and overall environmental impact. At the forefront of this technology is Revit, which integrates AI analytics to simulate building performance.
By analyzing weather patterns and daylighting, Revit AI tools not only optimize energy use but also help in select sustainable materials and reduce carbon footprints. This holistic approach ensures that designs are both efficient and environmentally responsible, paving the way for smarter, greener buildings.
5. Architectural Visualization and Rendering
The integration of AI into architectural visualization profoundly bridged the gap between ideas and reality. Through AI-powered tools, architects are able to produce highly realistic images and virtual walkthroughs in very little time compared to using traditional software.
For example, d5 render is a real-time rendering tool integrated with AI features that equip any-level designer with unparalleled photorealism and efficiency. It’s also important to highlight the various virtual reality and augmented reality applications in architecture that offer immersive experiences for both the designer and the client. This interactive approach not only aids in understanding spatial relationships and design impacts but also leads to better decision-making and higher client satisfaction.
Challenges of AI in Architecture
1. Creative Limitations – How original are AI-generated designs?
AI often provides a solution that may seem optimal, so some architects could accept it without doubt or consider alternative approaches. In one way or another, this leads most designers to develop an over-reliance on AL-generated solutions, which potentially leads to a decline in critical thinking and creative exploration. This is especially true given that despite AI’s exceptional ability to develop optimal designs based on specific parameters, it struggles greatly with open-ended creativity.
AI’s focus on efficiency and performance based on predefined criteria, overpowers the cultural and artistic nuances that human architects bring to the design. This might lead to a loss in architectural diversity and exacerbate the dilemma of global designs, where buildings all around the world look very similar.
2. Data Quality and Bias Issues – Why are we blindly trusting the AI database?
AI is only as valuable as the quality of the data it has been fed. So, poor or incomplete data will absolutely lead to flawed designs and decisions. It’s very important to be selective about what type of AI tool is being used. For example, architects must ensure that the developers of the tool are reputable and are keeping their AI up to date.
In this context comes a greater challenge, the AI data selection bias. On what base does the AI make suggestions? AI systems can unintentionally perpetuate biases present in their training data, leading to discriminatory outcomes in architectural design. This could result in spaces that do not cater to all demographics, which exacerbates social inequalities?. It is vital to include diverse datasets and regularly audit AI algorithms to ensure they produce fair and inclusive designs.
3. Technology Adoption – Why aren’t all architects using AI?
For some architects, adopting AI tools requires a dramatic shift in mindset and a willingness to embrace new ways of working. AI technology is intimidating and can pose a learning curve for newcomers. An interesting survey conducted by Chaos showed that when it comes to adopting AI tools in architecture, 25% of architects reported a challenge in the learning curve, 19% in it being an overwhelming choice, and 37% highlighted the lack of suitable training resources. So, it’s safe to assume that 81% of the respondents voiced the major issue of navigating the complexities of AI adoption. Notably, there is a scarcity of effective training and valuable educational resources for the smooth onboarding toward this technology.
If you are interested in learning more about how AI can be used in architecture, please check PAACADEMY’s AI workshops.
Ethical Considerations of AI in Architecture
1. Job Displacement
This has been the fear for a while, will AI take my job? Technically speaking, yes, of course it will, but not really. It’s totally valid to have thoughts about whether a mere “smart” machine deserves a job more than a human, but maybe this machine is taking away jobs that waste people’s time – it’s definitely an ethical debate. However, job di splacement is a major issue when companies do not provide resources to train their employees on the various uses of AI tools in architecture. Other than that, AI is taking all the repetitive tasks from the way such as drafting, and clearing the space for creative thinking.
Read more on how to become an AI Architect.
2. Data Privacy
The use of AI in architecture often involves the collection and processing of vast amounts of data, including personal and environmental data. This raises significant privacy concerns, as unauthorized access or misuse of this data could lead to serious breaches of privacy. Also poses the question, whose work is this AI copying, or is it even allowed to access it? Another serious concern arises with the increasing integration of architectural projects with AI, the risk of cyber attacks.
3. Environmental Impact
AI tools in architecture can phenomenally drive the creation of sustainable buildings and cities. However, the irony is that running or training AI-intensive models can gravely harm the environment.
For example, the GPUs that trained GPT-3 are reported to have consumed 1,300 megawatt-hours of electricity which is equivalent to the monthly electricity usage of around 1,450 average U.S. households. Another study by MIT estimated that training a deep learning model could result in 626,000 pounds of CO2 emissions. This is nearly five times the emissions produced by an average car over its entire lifecycle – including its manufacturing.
On a brighter note, sustainable efforts are in the making to minimize the environmental impact of AI. For instance, MIT is developing a network system that can reduce 1/1,300 CO2 emissions from training AI models in comparison to traditional methods.
Read more on how artificial intelligence can revolutionize urban health for proactive well-being.
The Future of AI in Architecture
AI is a force to reckon with, especially in the field of architecture, as it is elevating the profession to new heights. The following 8 points summarize all the ways AI will impact the future of architecture:
- Automate Design Processes
- Enhance Sustainability
- Design Smarter Resilient Cities
- Optimize Building Performance
- Foster Collaboration between architects, engineers,and data scientists.
- Improve Construction Efficiency
- Enhance Virtual Design and Construction
- Promote Innovation
Helsinki plans to be carbon-neutral by 2030 and is leveraging AI-powered energy management systems to achieve its goal. AI and robotics will revolutionize construction by automating complex building tasks. AI will further optimize energy consumption and environmental impact with innovations such as self-healing materials– combined with AI’s predictive analytics– which will help architects design structures that are not only sustainable but also resilient to environmental challenges.? In Virginia Tech’s Innovation Campus, SmithGroup utilized AI with computational algorithms to create a carbon-neutral building that optimizes energy through its photovoltaic skin. The possibilities are boundless.
AI is slowly becoming more than a tool, but rather a collaborative partner. Architects are now heralding an era of exceptional imagination, achieving the impossible by guiding AI to create wonders. AI will never replace architects, because even the most brilliant orchestra falls silent without its maestro.
One very interesting debate arises when you compare the integration of AI in architecture to Juhani Pallasmaa’s “The Thinking Hand”. It’s a brilliant book to read, but in essence it talks about how a mere pencil in the hand of an artist or architect becomes the bridge between imagination and the physical world; which ultimately leads to creative outcomes. So, the question begs, are we still in deep sensory connection with our creations while using various AI tools; if not, how else are we producing such marvelous innovations?

Smart city
A smart city is an urban model that leverages technology, human capital, and governance to improve sustainability, efficiency, and social inclusion, which are considered goals for cities of the future.[1] Smart cities use digital technology to collect data and operate services.[2][3] Data is collected from citizens, devices, buildings, or cameras. Smart city applications are diverse and include, but are not limited to, traffic and transportation systems,[4] power plants, utilities, urban forestry,[5] water supply networks, waste disposal, criminal investigations, information systems, schools, libraries, hospitals, and other community services.[6][7] The foundation of a smart city is built on the integration of people, technology, and processes, which connect and interact across sectors such as healthcare, transportation, education, infrastructure, etc.[8] Smart cities are characterized by the ways in which their local governments monitor, analyze, plan, and govern the city. In a smart city, data sharing extends to businesses, citizens, and other third parties who can derive benefit from using that data.[9][10] The three largest sources of spending associated with smart cities as of 2022 were visual surveillance, public transit, and outdoor lighting.[11]
Smart cities integrate Information and Communication Technologies (ICT), and devices connected to the Internet of Things (IOT) network to optimize city services and connect to citizens.[12][13][14] ICT can enhance the quality, performance, and interactivity of urban services, reduce costs and resource consumption, and to increase contact between citizens and government.[15] Smart city applications manage urban flows and allow for real-time responses.[16] A smart city may be more prepared to respond to challenges than one with a conventional "transactional" relationship with its citizens.[17][18] Yet, the term is open to many interpretations.[19] Many cities have already adopted some sort of smart city technology.
Smart city initiatives have been criticized as driven by corporations,[20][21] poorly adapted to residents' needs,[22][23] as largely unsuccessful,[citation needed] and as a move toward totalitarian surveillance.[24]
Background
[edit]Historically, cities functioned as centers of innovation, and the advent of the digital era presented opportunities and challenges to apply technology to create urban environments that are more efficient, sustainable, and livable.[25][26][27][28][how?][29]
The shift to smart cities necessitates a comprehensive restructuring of city management and operations, leading citizen participation, and methods of public service delivery.[28]
Cities seek to upgrade their infrastructure and service delivery to promote social inclusion, technological adoption, and economic development.[29][30][31][28]
The transformation into a smart city involves modifications in planning, management, and operational processes.[32] This data can subsequently be analyzed to identify areas for improvement and optimize urban services.
Information and communication technologies
[edit]The concept of smart cities emerged from global cities' adoption[33] of information and communications technologies.[34][35]
ICTs present challenges given financial limitations, technical obstacles, and privacy and security concerns. ICTs are also not uniformly accessible across communities, contributing to the digital divide.[29]
Definition
[edit]No commonly accepted definition of "smart city" has emerged.[25][36]: 71 Evaluating smart city initiatives is difficult without agreement on parameters. It also hampers the ability to compare projects and identify best practices.[37][38][32]
Deakin and Al Waer list four factors that contribute to the definition of a smart city:[39]
- Application of a wide range of electronic and digital technologies
- Use of ICT in living and working environments
- Use of ICT in government systems
- The territorialisation of practices that bring ICT and people together to enhance innovation and knowledge.
Deakin defines the smart city as one that uses ICT to meet the demands of the market (the citizens of the city), based on community involvement.[40] Studies of smart city projects can be used as an alternative to difficult-to-define broad definitions in order to clarify what smart cities are.[25][41]
Early definitions
[edit]Notable disparities among smart city definitions include the relative focus on economic advantages versus environmental or social benefits and specific technology choices.[29]
Smart city definitions include:
- Caragliu et al. (2011): "A city is smart when investments in human and social capital and traditional (transport) and modern (ICT) communication infrastructure fuel sustainable economic growth and a high quality of life, with a wise management of natural resources, through participatory governance."[42]
- Bakici, Almirall, & Wareham (2013): "Smart city as a high-tech intensive and advanced city that connects people, information, and city elements using new technologies in order to create a sustainable, greener city, competitive and innovative commerce, and an increased life quality."[43]
- Nam and Pardo (2011): "A smart city infuses information into its physical infrastructure to improve conveniences, facilitate mobility, add efficiencies, conserve energy, improve the quality of air and water, identify problems and fix them quickly, recover rapidly from disasters, collect data to make better decisions, deploy resources effectively, and share data to enable collaboration across entities and domains."[44]
Research
[edit]The main issues surrounding smart city research include:[45]
- Absence of intellectual exchange among researchers;
- Researcher inclination to pursue subjective avenues of research in isolation from their peers;
- The resulting division within the scientific community.
Motivations
[edit]Population growth
[edit]An important motivation for smart cities is projected population growth. The UN forecasts the global population to reach 9.6 to 13.2 billion by 2100, with cities absorbing 80% of this growth.[46]
Tragedy of the commons
[edit]An important goal of smart city initiatives is to use ICTs to address the tragedy of the commons problem.[how?] This phenomenon occurs when individuals acting in their own self-interest deplete a communal resource. For example, while each individual driver in a city saves time and flexibility by driving, the resultant excessive driving of the community causes traffic congestion and environmental issues. This situation is worsened when public transportation services get little attention due to the use of personal vehicles.[47]
History
[edit]Philosophical predecessors of smart cities can be found in utopian works such as New Atlantis (1626).[48] Another was Ebenezer Howard's 1898 concept of Garden Cities.[45] These were dense, size-limited cities founded in rural areas by private groups, combining the benefits of the city and the country.[49] Other conceptions include those of Edward Bellamy, Frank Lloyd Wright, and Le Corbusier.[45][49] Critics of smart cities draw parallels between the weaknesses of these utopian visions and the weaknesses of smart cities today.[45]
The concept of "smart cities" emerged from global cities' adoption of information and communications technologies for urban use, which can be used to improve efficiency, sustainability, and livability in urban environments.[33][34][35] Some of the earliest interventions in urban planning include the use of computational statistical analysis by the Community Analysis Bureau in Los Angeles in the late 1960s[50] and the establishment by Singapore of the National Computer Board in 1981.[51]
The smart city concept experienced a major surge around 2005. Tech companies sought to create information systems to enhance operational efficiency for cities.[52][53][54][55]
A global movement emerged advocating smart cities.[citation needed]
IBM launched its Smarter Planet marketing initiative in 2008,[56] which included the IBM Smarter Cities Challenge. In 2010, Cisco Systems, with $25 million from the Clinton Foundation, established its Connected Urban Development program in partnership with San Francisco, Amsterdam, and Seoul. In 2011, the Smart City Expo World Congress in Barcelona attracted 6000 people from 50 countries. The European Commission in 2012 established the Smart Cities Marketplace, a centralized hub for urban initiatives in the European Union.[57] The 2015 Chancellor's Budget for the United Kingdom proposed to invest £140 million in smart cities and IoT.[58] Smart city competitions were launched in the 2010s by Bloomberg Philanthropies, the Rockefeller Foundation, and the United States Department of Transportation.[21] In 2016, AT&T launched an alliance with Cisco, Deloitte, Ericsson, General Electric, IBM, Intel, and Qualcomm, with municipal partners Atlanta, Georgia; Chicago, Illinois; and Dallas, Texas.[21]
Characteristics
[edit]Key characteristics that define innovative urban environments include:[59]
- Connectivity: IOT networks collect and transmit data from sensors throughout the urban environment.[60]
- Data-driven decision making: Advanced analytics and artificial intelligence enable more informed and responsive governance.[61]
- Sustainable infrastructure: Energy-efficient buildings, renewable energy, and intelligent transportation systems.[62]
- Urban Optimization: Reduce resource usage, reduce ecological footprints, and enhance living standards to create more environmentally responsible urban spaces.[63]
- Citizen engagement: Facilitate communication between residents and government, promoting participation in urban planning and decision-making processes.[64]
- Smart mobility: Integrate public transit, bike-sharing, and autonomous vehicles, aim to reduce congestion and improve accessibility,[65] as well as analyzing mobility behavioral patterns of citizens to improve services and optimize the city infrastructure.[66]
- Enhanced public services: Improve the delivery of essential services.[67]
A 2024 research article identified six spatial manifestations of the smart cities, which are considered expressions of 21st century capitalism:[68]
- Science parks and smart campuses;
- Innovation districts;
- Smart neighborhoods;
- City-wide, metropolitan and city-regional smart city initiatives;
- Urban platforms;
- Alternative smart city spaces.
Methods
[edit]Information and communications technologies
[edit]It has been suggested that a smart city (or other community) uses information technologies to:[citation needed]
- Make more efficient use of physical infrastructure (roads, built environment, and other physical assets) through artificial intelligence and data analytics in order to support a strong and healthy economic, social, cultural development.[20]
- Engage effectively with local governance[69] by use of open innovation processes and e-participation, improving the collective intelligence of the city's institutions through e-governance,[16] with emphasis placed on citizen participation and co-design.[70][71]
- Learn, adapt, and innovate and thereby respond more effectively and promptly to changing circumstances by improving the intelligence of the city.[16][72]
They evolve towards a strong integration of all dimensions of human intelligence, collective intelligence, and also artificial intelligence within the city.[73]: 112–113 [74] According to Mitchell, the intelligence of cities "resides in the increasingly effective combination of digital telecommunication networks (the nerves), ubiquitously embedded intelligence (the brain), sensors and tags (the sensory organs), and software (the knowledge and cognitive competence)".[75]
The physical components of IT systems are crucial to early-stage smart city development. Wired infrastructure is required to support the IOT and wireless technologies central to more interconnected living.[76] A wired city environment provides general access to continually updated digital and physical infrastructure. The latest in telecommunications, robotics, IOT, and various connected technologies can then be deployed to support human capital and productivity.[77][78]
Forms of intelligence
[edit]Intelligence in smart cities has been demonstrated in three ways:[citation needed]
- Orchestration intelligence:[16] Cities establish institutions and community-based problem solving and collaborations, such as in Bletchley Park, where the Nazi Enigma cipher was decoded by a team led by Alan Turing. This has been referred to as the first example of a smart city or an intelligent community.[79]
- Empowerment intelligence: Cities provide open platforms, experimental facilities and smart city infrastructure in order to cluster innovation in certain districts. These are seen in the Kista Science City in Stockholm and the Cyberport Zone in Hong Kong. Similar facilities have also been established in Melbourne and Kyiv.[80]
- Instrumentation intelligence: City infrastructure is made smart through real-time data collection, with analysis and predictive modelling across city districts. There is much controversy surrounding this, particularly with regards to surveillance issues in smart cities.
Examples of instrumentation intelligence are those implemented in Amsterdam.[81] This is realized through:[16]
- A common IP infrastructure that is open to researchers to develop applications.
- Wireless meters and devices transmit information at the point in time.
- A number of homes being provided with smart energy meters to become aware of energy consumption and reduce energy usage.
- Solar power garbage compactors, car recharging stations and energy saving lamps.
Energy usage
[edit]Smart cities use data and technology to create efficiencies, improve sustainability, create economic development, and enhance quality of life factors for people living and working in the city.[citation needed] A variety of different datasets may need to be integrated to create a smart energy infrastructure.[82] Employment of smart technologies enables the more efficient application of integrated energy technologies in the city allowing the development of more self-sustaining areas or even positive energy districts that produce more energy than they consume.[83][how?]
A smart city is powered by "smart connections" for various items such as street lighting, smart buildings, distributed energy resources, data analytics, and smart transportation. Amongst these things, energy is paramount; this is why utility companies play a key role in smart cities. Electric companies, working partnership with city officials, technology companies and a number of other institutions, are among the major players that helped accelerate the growth of America's smart cities.[84]
According to David K. Owens, the former executive vice president of the Edison Electric Institute, two key elements that a smart city must have are an integrated communications platform and a "dynamic resilient grid."[85]
Smart grids are an important technology in smart cities. The improved flexibility of the smart grid permits greater penetration of highly variable renewable energy sources such as solar power and wind power.[citation needed]
Energy Data Management Systems can help to save cities energy by recording data and using it to increase efficiency.[86]
Data management
[edit]For a smart city to function, it is necessary for it to manage an enormous amount of data collected through the embedded devices and systems in its environment.[87] This is also important for the cities growth and security.[88] Smart cities use a variety of data collection, processing, and disseminating technologies, in conjunction with data security and privacy measures, in attempting to encourage innovation and improve citizens' quality of life.[87] This can relate to topics including utilities, health, transportation, entertainment, and government services.[87]
Online collaborative sensor data management platforms are on-line database services that allow sensor owners to register and connect their devices to feed data into an on-line database for storage and allow developers to connect to the database and build their own applications based on that data.[89][90]
Electronic cards (known as smart cards) are another common component in smart city contexts. These cards possess a unique encrypted identifier that allows the owner to log into a range of government-provided services (or e-services) without setting up multiple accounts. The single identifier allows governments to aggregate data about citizens and their preferences to improve the provision of services and to determine common interests of groups. This technology has been implemented in Southampton.[39]
Cognitive technologies, such as artificial intelligence and machine learning, can be trained on the data generated by connected city devices to identify patterns. The efficacy and impact of particular policy decisions can be quantified by cognitive systems studying the continuous interactions of humans with their urban surroundings.[91]
Transportation
[edit]Bicycle-sharing systems are an important element in smart cities.[92]
Intelligent transportation systems and CCTV systems are also being developed.[93]
Retractable bollards can restrict access inside city centers (i.e. to delivery trucks resupplying outlet stores). Opening and closing of such barriers is traditionally done manually, through an electronic pass[94] but can even be done by means of automatic number-plate recognition cameras connected to the bollard system.[95]
Human factors
[edit]According to McKinsey, smart city initiatives can have measurable positive impacts on the quality of life of its citizens and visitors.[96] The human framework of a smart city – its economy, knowledge networks, and human support systems – is an important indicator of its success.[97]
For example, arts and culture initiatives are common focus areas in smart city planning.[98][99] Innovation is associated with intellectual curiosity and creativeness, and various projects have demonstrated that knowledge workers participate in a diverse mix of cultural and artistic activities.[100][101]
Since mobility is a key area of smart city development, building a capable workforce through education initiatives is necessary.[97][clarification needed] A city's learning capacity includes its education system, including available workforce training and support, and its cultural development and exchange.[102]
Numerous smart city programs also focus on soft infrastructure development, like increasing access to voluntary organizations and designated safe zones.[103] This focus on social and relational capital means diversity, inclusion, and ubiquitous access to public services is worked into city planning.[78]
The development of a knowledge economy is also central to smart city projects.[104] Smart cities seeking to be hubs of economic activity in emerging tech and service sectors stress the value of innovation in city development.[78]
Enabling technologies
[edit]Smart cities leverage a number of technologies:
- Mobile devices (such as smartphones and tablets) are a key technology allowing citizens to connect to the smart city services.[105][106][107]
- Smart homes and specifically, the technology used in them, contribute data and connection to smart cities as a whole.[108][109][110][111][112]
- Digital libraries have been established in several cities,[113][114][115][116][117][118] and contribute to the dissemination of information within and across cities.
Additional supporting technology and trends include remote work,[119][120][121] telehealth,[122][123] the blockchain,[124][125] and online banking technology,[126]
A "ubiquitous city"(U-city) is one concept of a smart city that provides access to public services through any connected device, bringing easy accessibility to every infrastructure.[127]
Criticism
[edit]Criticisms of smart cities include:[20]
- The use of big data collection and analytics raises questions over surveillance in smart cities, particularly over predictive policing.
- Over-emphasis on smart cities means ignoring other domains.[128]
- Urban development is often haphazard. A data-based approach "can deaden and stupefy the people who live in its all-efficient embrace".[129]
- Technological and networked infrastructures have downsides that may offset the benefits.[130]
- The capital mobility that allows business to take advantage of smart cities also allows them to leave for a better offer.[20]
- Urban data collection involves surveillance, which potentially invades individual privacy. Without adequate protections, data collection practices like scanning, identification, location tracking (including time and direction) can empower bad actors.[131]
- Smart city approaches are irrelevant to cities without the means to implement the required technologies, such as in developing countries.[22]
- Persons with disabilities are not always accommodated by smart city technologies.[23]
- Digital technologies can have a significant environmental footprint that may be visited onto other communities.[132][133][134]
- "Smart city" can be used as a slogan merely to stimulate land revenue generation.[135]
- Smart city technologies that are adopted tend to be those that deliver digital services directly to residents (e.g., ride-hailing services and online food ordering) or which solve a specific problem of municipal government, rather than enhancing infrastructure.[21]
- Digital technology has the potential to be used in negative as well as positive ways, and its use is inherently political.[69]
- Smart cities can perpetuate or mitigate inequalities[136][137][138][139][140]
In 2023, the Salisbury smart cities controversy arose after a proposal for smart city technology in the South Australian local government area of City of Salisbury was met with community backlash leading to a range of conspiracy theories.[141][142][143]
Initiatives
[edit]Africa
[edit]The African Union Commission pledged to utilize ICTs to advance sustainable urban development.[144]
Canada
[edit]The "smart communities" movement took shape as a strategy to involve more users in IT.[102] Primary issues included traffic congestion, school overcrowding and air pollution.[102]
China
[edit]China's smart cities movement began with a pilot program launched in 2012 through its Ministry of Housing and Urban-Rural Development.[36]: 58–59 China's National New-Type Urbanization Plan for 2014-2020 included smart cities.[36]: 59–60 It identified six important aspects for developing smart cities:[36]: 60
- information network and broadband
- digitization of planning management
- smart infrastructure
- convenience of public services
- modernizing industrial development
- sophisticated social governance.
As of 2016, approximately 500 smart city projects had launched.[36]: 59 In 2021, China took first in all categories of the International AI City Challenge – "by some estimates, China has half of the world's smart cities".[145]
Commercial companies
[edit]Alibaba created City Brain.[146][147] Its first overseas implementation began in 2018 in Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia.[148]: 82
Baidu developed Apollo, a self-driving technology.[149] Tencent launched medical technology,[149] such as WeChat Intelligent Healthcare, Tencent Doctorwork, and AI Medical Innovation System (AIMIS).[150]
As of 2021, Huawei and ZTE had more than 350 smart city projects in more than 60 countries.[151]: 11
As of 2024, "Safe City" digital products were marketed abroad by Chinese companies including Dahua Technology, Huawei, ZTE, and Hikvision.[148]: 80 Huawei's Safe City Compact Solution focuses on improving safety.[152][153][154] In 2018, Serbia announced a Safe City project for Belgrade in conjunction with Huawei, using one thousand cameras with advanced facial recognition and license plate recognition capabilities.[148]: 82
Europe
[edit]EU members began working on smart city developments and ICT initiatives in the mid-2010s. The Digital Agenda for Europe framework emphasizes harnessing ICTs. The 2014-15 budget of the Horizon 2020 Research and Innovation program, included approximately 200 million Euros to expedite smart cities.[155][156]: 337–355 [157]
As of 2024 Estonia had proceeded furthest towards digitizing public services.
India
[edit]The Smart Cities Mission is a retrofitting and urban renewal program spearheaded by the Ministry of Urban Development.[158]
Southeast Asia
[edit]ASEAN Smart Cities Network (ASCN) is a collaborative platform to advance smart city efforts across ASEAN by catalysing bankable projects, and securing funding and support from ASEAN's external partners.[159]
United Nations
[edit]The New Urban Agenda emphasized the importance of smart city development, establishing a fundamental commitment for the UN's 193 member states.[160][161][162]
United States
[edit]The United States allocated more than $160 million toward smart city initiatives. Challenges include traffic congestion, economic growth, crime, climate change, and public services.[citation needed]
Taiwan
[edit]Taiwan has actively promoted smart city development through government-led initiatives such as the Smart City Taiwan program, launched in 2018 by the Ministry of Economic Affairs. The program partners with local governments and private companies to deploy pilot projects in areas including healthcare, transportation, public safety, environmental monitoring, and education.[163]
Taipei,[164] Taoyuan,[165] and Tainan[166] have emerged as leading smart cities, implementing solutions such as AI-assisted traffic management, smart street lighting, and real-time air quality sensors. The government also supports a Public-Private-People Partnership (P-P-P-P) model to involve citizens in the co-creation of services.[167]
Taiwan's approach emphasizes the integration of 5G, IOT, and artificial intelligence (AI) to improve urban livability and sustainability. The annual Smart City Summit & Expo in Taipei has become a major platform for showcasing Taiwan's innovations and fostering international collaboration.[168][169]
Implementation
[edit]The most common characteristics of a "smart city" are networked infrastructure; emphasis on business-led urban development; social inclusion of various resident groups; and an emphasis on the environment.[25][29]
Partnerships
[edit]Smart city initiatives require collaboration and involvement from government agencies, businesses, community organizations, academia, and citizens. Collaborating with businesses and academia brings technical know-how and research capabilities.[170][171][172]
Collaborations with community organizations can improve equity and inclusivity.[29]

A systematic review on artificial intelligence applications in architecture
DOI:
https://doi.org/10.47818/DRArch.2023.v4i1085Keywords:
algorithm, architectural design, architecture, artificial intelligence, computitional designAbstract
Since the advent and usage of artificial intelligence approaches in architecture, a significant number of studies have focused on integrating technological solutions to architectural issues. Artificial intelligence applications in architectural design range from intelligent material design to architectural plan solutions. The ubiquity and distribution of research in this field, as well as the rising use of artificial intelligence techniques to solve design challenges, require an analytical classification of the essential literature review. This article presents a descriptive and analytical review of the work on artificial intelligence applications in architecture. A strong review has been made that identifies and addresses the gaps in artificial intelligence and architecture; and the literature review is transformed into statistical plots. The study's findings indicate a growing interest in artificial intelligence in the field of architecture. There is a need for novel research to be conducted in these areas using advanced technology and techniques.

Artificial intelligence (AI) is redefining how cities are planned, designed, and managed. Urban planners, architects, and policymakers are increasingly using AI for urban planning to develop efficient, adaptive, and sustainable environments. Through machine learning, computational design, and generative modeling, cities are evolving into responsive systems that align spatial development with social and environmental conditions.
This article examines how AI contributes to urban planning and architecture. It outlines the key applications of digital twins, predictive analytics, generative design, and AI-assisted visualization. Each section focuses on practical implementations and their impact on mobility, sustainability, and design efficiency.
What is Urban Planning?
Urban planning is the process of designing, organizing, and managing the growth and function of cities, towns, and regions. It combines ideas from architecture, geography, transportation, environmental science, economics, and public policy to create places that are efficient, healthy, and enjoyable to live in.
At its heart, urban planning focuses on how land and space are used. It involves deciding where people will live, work, study, and spend their free time, as well as how they will move between those places. Planners make choices about zoning, housing, transportation networks, public services, and green spaces, always aiming to balance development with environmental protection and social well-being.
Good urban planning seeks to use land efficiently and encourage compact, connected growth. It provides accessible transportation options and strong infrastructure that supports mobility and reduces congestion. It promotes affordable and inclusive housing, protects natural areas, and ensures that communities are resilient to climate change and other challenges.
In practice, urban planners rely on data, maps, and community input to guide their decisions. They work with governments, developers, and residents to define a shared vision for how a city should evolve over time. This may include revitalizing older neighborhoods, improving public transit systems, or designing new areas for sustainable development.
The Role of Artificial Intelligence in Urban Planning
AI for urban planning integrates data analytics, machine learning, and computational modeling to improve responsiveness in urban systems. Traditional masterplans often remain static and struggle to adapt to rapid change. AI introduces predictive systems that process real-time data from sensors, mobility networks, and environmental monitors, enabling cities to anticipate challenges rather than respond retrospectively.
AI-driven diffusion models analyze datasets related to population, mobility, and land use, offering planners data-supported tools for housing and infrastructure management.
Deep learning and computer vision techniques interpret satellite imagery to monitor urban morphology, building density, and vegetation change, improving spatial awareness.
Generative architecture and parametric methods create adaptive forms that optimize structure, performance, and environmental efficiency through iterative computation.
Parametric design establishes dynamic relationships between geometric and environmental parameters, allowing automated design adjustments.
Computational design workflows integrate coding into architectural processes, reducing manual modeling errors and enhancing precision.
Predictive design systems simulate energy consumption and comfort levels before construction, supporting informed sustainability decisions.
Neural networks model material behavior under different conditions and use neural rendering techniques to simulate realistic responses, optimizing durability and energy performance.
These methods demonstrate how AI transforms planning and design into data-informed, adaptive processes focused on measurable performance outcomes.
How Are Urban Planners Using Artificial Intelligence?
Urban planners and landscape architects are adopting artificial intelligence to enhance spatial analysis, environmental forecasting, and design decision-making. AI supports multiple stages of planning, from site assessment to visualization, by processing complex datasets and generating predictive insights.
Predictive models analyze real-time data from IoT sensors and digital twins to anticipate mobility demand, environmental risks, and infrastructure needs.
Machine learning assists in land-use optimization, identifying underused areas and suggesting redevelopment strategies based on demographic and ecological indicators.
Computer vision detects urban expansion, vegetation loss, and landform changes using satellite and drone imagery, helping maintain ecological continuity within urban growth.
AI-based simulations model pedestrian movement, traffic flow, and microclimatic effects to support safe and efficient spatial organization.
Integrated platforms consolidate data from transportation, energy, and water systems to enable coordinated planning across disciplines.
In landscape architecture, AI-driven generative tools model topography, planting strategies, and water flow, allowing designers to balance functionality with environmental performance. These applications enable more evidence-based, adaptive, and resilient planning outcomes.
Data and Digital Infrastructure in Intelligent Cities
Data infrastructure forms the foundation of intelligent cities. AI depends on structured information gathered through IoT systems, building information modeling (BIM), and digital urban databases. These datasets enable predictive maintenance, energy optimization, and infrastructure management.
Digital twins replicate real-world conditions in virtual form, allowing planners to test interventions before implementation. AI enhances these models by predicting building performance, environmental impact, and energy use. Computational simulations further evaluate daylight, microclimate, and material behavior, contributing to climate-responsive and sustainable design outcomes.
Applications of AI in Urban Planning
AI for urban planning supports analysis, prediction, and coordinated action across city systems. The following use cases summarize current practice with clear, measurable outcomes.
Mobility and Transportation
Cities are becoming smarter by using real-time sensor feeds to optimize traffic signal timing and improve corridor flow. This reduces congestion, shortens travel times, and improves air quality. Transit agencies can forecast demand to adjust headways and deploy vehicles where they are needed most, creating a more responsive and efficient network.
Pedestrian movement modeling helps make streets safer by identifying risky crossings and optimizing signal phases for people on foot. At the same time, curb space, parking areas, and micromobility zones can be managed using predictive analytics that match supply with demand throughout the day. Collision hot spots can be identified in advance so that planners can test safety measures before they are implemented.
Environmental Sustainability and Utilities
Forecasting building and district energy loads supports grid balancing and helps cities move toward cleaner energy systems. Artificial intelligence can detect water leaks or pressure anomalies through pattern recognition, reducing both waste and maintenance costs. Waste collection routes and container placement are optimized to save time, fuel, and labor.
Urban heat islands are mapped to show where temperatures are most severe, helping cities plan for tree planting and surface cooling strategies. Rainfall and runoff simulations are used to prioritize stormwater management projects, ensuring that interventions deliver the greatest environmental and social benefits.
Land Use, Housing, and Growth Management
By analyzing satellite imagery and parcel data, cities can anticipate how land use will change over time. Planners can test zoning scenarios and evaluate their effects on accessibility, density, and public services. This makes it easier to balance growth with quality of life.
Housing forecasts identify needs by location, affordability level, and household type. Potential development sites are also screened for brownfield risks and remediation feasibility. To promote fairness, planners compare outcomes across neighborhoods and ensure that growth supports equity as well as efficiency.
Public Participation and Decision Support
Complex technical plans can be turned into visuals that everyone can understand. Chat-based tools make it easier to collect structured community feedback from large groups of residents. Natural language processing (NLP) helps summarize thousands of public comments and identify the themes that appear most often.
Interactive scenario tools let users see the effects of different choices in real time, while open dashboards track progress toward shared goals. Together, these methods make planning more transparent, accessible, and data-driven.
Safety, Resilience, and Climate Adaptation
Cities face rising risks from floods, fires, and landslides. By combining data from multiple sources, planners can generate detailed risk maps that guide land-use and emergency management decisions. Evacuation routes and emergency service coverage are simulated to test preparedness before a disaster occurs.
Critical facilities such as hospitals and utilities are prioritized for upgrades based on their exposure to hazards. Cooling, shading, and ventilation strategies are tested to improve heat resilience, while green infrastructure projects are ranked for their overall benefits to people and the environment.
Operations, Assets, and Lifecycle Management
Predictive maintenance models help cities manage infrastructure more efficiently. Pavement and bridge deterioration can be forecasted, allowing repairs to be scheduled at the optimal time. Drone and street-level imagery automate inspections and provide continuous monitoring.
Repair plans take into account cost, condition, and the level of disruption to the public. Construction progress can be tracked to detect deviations early, and Building Information Modeling (BIM) can be linked with asset management systems to keep records up to date throughout the life of a project.
Design, Visualization, and Permitting
Modern design tools can generate multiple concept variants that meet requirements for light, wind, and massing. Digital twins simulate construction phasing and logistics, helping teams plan efficiently and minimize disruption.
Permit applications are pre-checked for code compliance using rule-based systems that reduce delays. Designers evaluate façade materials and shading choices for environmental performance. Three-dimensional visualizations and interactive explainers make complex information clear for boards, stakeholders, and the general public.
Through these applications, cities can reduce congestion, cut emissions, expand housing options, and build public trust through transparency. Urban planners gain access to faster evidence, clearer communication, and more rigorous scenario testing. These capabilities lead to better-aligned policies, stronger investments, and communities that are more resilient and sustainable.
AI in Urban Design, Visualization, and Rendering
AI has expanded the scope of urban design from static representation to dynamic, data-driven visualization. It supports architects and planners in concept generation, rendering, and performance simulation, improving workflow precision and speed.
Generative AI Architecture and Concept Development
Generative AI platforms analyze site parameters such as sunlight, airflow, and circulation to produce optimized design alternatives.
AI systems combine environmental and performance data to generate design scenarios efficiently.
Generative tools translate environmental conditions into spatial solutions that align comfort, energy, and functionality.
Machine learning introduces adaptability, enabling forms that evolve with external conditions.
Algorithmic and parametric design tools create relationships between geometry and performance, automating modifications and reducing design time. This approach supports adaptive, performance-oriented architectural processes.
AI-Assisted Visualization and Rendering
AI-based rendering technologies have significantly accelerated design communication. Real-time neural rendering minimizes manual effort while maintaining visual accuracy.
AI rendering platforms such as D5 Render, Lumion AI Mode, and Stable Diffusion create photorealistic images from sketches or text prompts.
Neural rendering reproduces textures, materials, and lighting with high fidelity.
Automation enables faster visualization and reduces dependency on manual post-production processes.
ArchiVinci: AI Visualization Ecosystem for Architects and Urban Designers
ArchiVinci is an AI platform that integrates architectural visualization and urban design workflows. It combines concept generation, visualization, and rendering in a unified environment where users can transform sketches or 3D models into realistic outputs within seconds.
AI Urban Design Modules
At the urban scale, ArchiVinci includes specialized tools for planners and designers working on masterplans, landscapes, and city-scale proposals.
Masterplan AI module transforms schematic site plans into detailed, colored aerial views with zoning, materials, and circulation layers. It supports the visualization of development strategies and urban morphologies.
Landscape Design AI module generates exterior scenes such as parks, plazas, and open spaces by interpreting spatial layouts and textual prompts. It visualizes planting, materials, and lighting for outdoor environments.
These modules facilitate the rapid communication of spatial ideas and support visual clarity in large-scale urban projects.
Comprehensive AI Rendering and Design Capabilities
ArchiVinci also provides a range of AI rendering tools that improve visualization quality across architectural and interior applications.
AI rendering and visualization functions produce high-quality imagery from sketches or early design models.
Exterior design and interior design generator convert basic geometry into contextual, photorealistic renders.
Render enhancement (upscaling) increase clarity and realism for professional presentation.
Panoramic and cinematic output transforms static images into immersive environments and animations.
AI Architecture Concept and Staging Tools
Architecture AI like ArchiVinci supports conceptual and post-production workflows through dedicated modules.
Style and concept generation allows the reinterpretation of designs in various architectural styles. The integrated Style Transfer AI module adjusts materials and aesthetics across interior and exterior contexts.
Virtual staging and Inpainting AI modules enable refurnishing, renovation visualization, and façade modification for adaptive reuse scenarios.
Smart editing tools assist with texture mapping, lighting correction, and generating consistent camera perspectives for design reviews.
Through these features, ArchiVinci demonstrates how AI-based visualization can contribute to communication, iteration, and evaluation in architectural and urban contexts.
Human-AI Co-Design and Architectural Creativity
AI contributes to collaborative design by combining analytical precision with creative interpretation. Human-AI co-design methods integrate computational generation with human evaluation.
Designers provide inputs, constraints, and feedback while AI processes complex geometries and scenarios.
Generative models learn spatial relationships and proportions, producing alternatives that can be refined by human decision-making.
The process enhances design diversity while maintaining efficiency and technical accuracy.
Impacts on Urban Visualization and Design Communication
AI-based visualization improves the communication of urban projects to decision-makers and the public.
Interactive renderings and real-time simulations allow users to explore performance and spatial impact visually.
Virtual and augmented reality experiences present proposals at an immersive scale.
Integration of analytical data within visuals increases understanding of design implications.
The Future of AI-Enabled Visualization
Advancements in deep learning and reinforcement learning are expected to shift visualization from static imagery toward behavioral simulation.
AI will simulate dynamic factors such as pedestrian movement, lighting variation, and environmental comfort.
Responsive architecture will adapt configurations based on feedback from real-time data.
Cloud-based rendering will expand accessibility to high-quality visualization across disciplines.
AI for Urban Resilience and Sustainability
AI supports climate adaptation, disaster preparedness, and energy management. Predictive design systems assess microclimates, heat islands, and flood zones to inform spatial configuration. Machine learning models predict building energy demand and optimize performance.
Material optimization and structural analysis tools improve resource efficiency and reduce waste. AI-based disaster modeling assists in developing responsive infrastructure and emergency planning strategies.
The Future of AI-Driven Cities
AI-driven cities are expected to operate through data connectivity and automation. Urban systems will use robotics, analytics, and digital twins for continuous management of infrastructure. AI applications in construction will enhance precision, safety, and scheduling.
Responsive architecture will adjust lighting, ventilation, and form in response to environmental feedback. Human-AI collaboration will remain central, with planners and designers focusing on interpretation, ethics, and long-term strategy.
Key Takeaways
AI transforms urban planning into a data-driven and adaptive process, improving decision-making speed and accuracy. Real-time analysis makes cities more flexible and sustainable.
Digital twins and predictive models test infrastructure virtually to reduce risks and strengthen resilience. This approach optimizes resource use and minimizes environmental impact.
Generative and parametric design tools produce creative and efficient solutions through AI-driven optimization. They enhance speed, precision, and sustainability in architecture.
AI increases efficiency in energy, water, and waste management, reducing the environmental footprint of cities. Smart resource management supports sustainable urban development.
AI-powered visualization tools make complex plans easy to understand for everyone. This improves public participation and transparency in decision-making.
Human-AI collaboration enhances creativity while preserving ethical and cultural values. AI supports human intuition as a complementary design partner.
Ethical issues such as data privacy, bias, and fairness must be carefully managed. Transparent governance and inclusive data policies are key to responsible AI use.
Future urban professionals will need hybrid skills in design, data science, and ethics. This interdisciplinary approach will enable the creation of intelligent and equitable cities.
AI enables cities to become self-learning and self-optimizing systems. As a result, future urban environments will be safer, more efficient, and human-centered.
Frequently Asked Questions
How can AI in urban planning reinforce existing social inequalities?
AI systems often rely on historical data that may reflect existing biases in zoning, infrastructure, and demographics. Without proper auditing and inclusive datasets, predictive models can unintentionally reproduce or amplify these inequities though when designed responsibly, AI also has the potential to identify and help correct such imbalances.
Does AI threaten personal privacy in smart cities?
Yes. AI systems depend on extensive data collection from urban sensors, cameras, and mobile devices. While this enables better management of city systems, it also raises privacy concerns if safeguards and transparent governance are not in place to prevent misuse or over-surveillance.
What principles guide responsible AI use in urban contexts?
Responsible AI in urban planning should be guided by principles such as transparency, fairness, accountability, participation, and sustainability. Implementing these in practice requires clear governance frameworks, interdisciplinary collaboration, and ongoing evaluation to ensure ethical alignment.
How does AI improve efficiency in urban planning and design?
AI enhances planning efficiency by automating data analysis, simulation, and visualization tasks. Predictive models anticipate mobility demand, environmental risks, and infrastructure needs, allowing planners to allocate resources proactively rather than reactively. Computational design and digital twins reduce modeling time and increase decision accuracy.
What role do digital twins play in intelligent city management?
Digital twins replicate physical urban systems in virtual space, enabling real-time monitoring and simulation. When combined with AI, they help test interventions such as traffic flow adjustments or building performance before implementation. This leads to safer, more cost-effective, and sustainable outcomes.
How does AI influence architectural creativity?
AI expands creative potential by generating design alternatives based on environmental and performance data. Generative and parametric tools allow architects to experiment with adaptive forms while maintaining structural and energy efficiency. Human designers still provide aesthetic direction and judgment, creating a true human-AI co-design process.
What are the limitations of AI in urban design?
AI cannot fully replicate the contextual, cultural, and emotional understanding that human designers bring. It performs best when combined with human interpretation. Other limitations include data quality, system interoperability, and the risk of over-reliance on algorithmic outcomes.
How does AI impact construction and infrastructure management?
AI-driven predictive maintenance identifies early signs of infrastructure wear such as pavement deterioration or bridge stress, allowing timely repairs. It also improves construction safety, progress tracking, and cost estimation by analyzing drone imagery and sensor data.
How will AI shape the future of urban governance?
As cities become increasingly data-driven, AI will play a key role in automating services, monitoring sustainability goals, and informing policy. However, responsible governance must ensure transparency, citizen participation, and ethical oversight to prevent technocratic decision-making.
What skills will urban planners and architects need in the age of AI?
Future professionals will need hybrid expertise that blends urban design, data analytics, and computational modeling. Core architect skills will evolve to include coding, GIS, machine learning, and responsible AI literacy, while still relying on traditional planning competencies such as community engagement and spatial reasoning. These combined abilities will help designers navigate complex urban challenges with both technical precision and human centered insight.
How does AI enhance visualization and public communication?
AI-based visualization tools such as neural rendering and generative modeling transform complex plans into intuitive, realistic images. This improves communication with non-technical audiences, enabling stakeholders and citizens to better understand design proposals and their potential impacts.

Design computing
The terms design computing and other relevant terms including design and computation and computational design refer to the study and practice of design activities through the application and development of novel ideas and techniques in computing. One of the early groups to coin this term was the Key Centre of Design Computing and Cognition at the University of Sydney in Australia, which for more than fifty years (since the late 1960s) pioneered the research, teaching, and consulting of design and computational technologies. This group organised the academic conference series "Artificial Intelligence in Design (AID)"[1] published by Springer during that period. AID was later renamed "Design Computing and Cognition (DCC)"[2] and is currently a leading biannual conference in the field. Other notable groups in this area are the Design and Computation[3] group at Massachusetts Institute of Technology's School of Architecture + Planning and the Computational Design[4] group at Georgia Tech.
Whilst these terms share in general an interest in computational technologies and design activity, there are important differences in the various approaches, theories, and applications. For example, while in some circles the term "computational design" refers in general to the creation of new computational tools and methods in the context of computational thinking,[5] design computing is concerned with bridging these two fields in order to build an increased understanding of design.[6]
The Bachelor of Design Computing (BDesComp)[7] was created in 2003 at the University of Sydney and continues to be a leading programme in interaction design and creative technologies, now hosted by the Design Lab. In that context, design computing is defined to be the use and development of computational models of design processes and digital media to assist and/or automate various aspects of the design process with the goal of producing higher quality and new design forms.[8]
Areas
[edit]In recent years a number of research and education areas have been grouped under the umbrella term "design computing", namely:
- Artificial intelligence in design
- Expert systems and knowledge-based systems
- Computational creativity
- Computer-aided design
- Responsive computer-aided design
- Digital architecture
- Digital morphogenesis
- Visual and spatial modelling
- Computational analogy
- Automated design systems
- Design support systems
- Computer-supported cooperative work (CSCW)
- Building information modeling (BIM)
- Extended reality (XR)[9][10] and spatial computing
- Digital place-making[11]
Research groups
[edit]The main research groups working in this area span from Faculties of Architecture, Engineering and Computer Science. Australia has been a pioneer in this area. For the last five decades the Key Centre of Design Computing and Cognition (KCDC), currently known as the Design Lab, at the University of Sydney has been active in establishing this area of research and teaching. The University of Sydney offers a Bachelor of Design Computing ([1]) and the University of New South Wales also in Sydney a Bachelor of Computational Design ([2]). In the US this research area is also known as "Design and Computation", namely at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT). Other relevant research groups include:
- Critical Research in Digital Architecture (CRIDA), Faculty of Architecture, Building and Planning, University of Melbourne
- School of Architecture, Carnegie Mellon
- Department of Computer Science, University College London
- Department of Informatics Engineering, Universidade de Coimbra
- Department of Computer Science, Vrije University, Amsterdam
- Creativity and Cognition Studios, University of Technology Sydney
- Department of Computer Science, University of Colorado at Boulder
- Department of Architecture, Tokyo Institute of Technology
- Department of Architecture, MIT
- Department of Computer Science, Helsinki University of Technology
- College of Architecture, Georgia Institute of Technology
- Design Machine Group, University of Washington College of Built Environments, Seattle
- Design Computing Program, Georgia Institute of Technology College of Architecture
- School of Interactive Arts + Technology, Simon Fraser University
- Department of Architecture, Technical University of Delft, The Netherlands
- Institute of Computational Design, University of Stuttgart
- Architectural Design Computing, Istanbul Technical University
- Faculty of Architecture, Istanbul Bilgi University, Turkey
- Centre of IT and Architecture (CITA), The Royal Danish Academy of Fine Arts, Copenhagen
- Institute of Architectural Algorithms & Applications (Inst.AAA), Southeast University, Nanjing
- Department of Experimental and Digital Design and Construction, University of Kassel, Germany
- Computational Media Design (CMD) program, University of Calgary, Canada
- School of Civil Engineering, Architecture and Urbanism (FEC-Unicamp), University of Campinas, Brazil
- Computation, Appearance, and Manufacturing group (CAM), Max Planck Institute for Informatics, Saarbrücken, Germany
Conferences
[edit]The biannual International Conference on Design Computing and Cognition (DCC) brings together high quality research on this area, as do annual conferences by the Association for Computer Aided Design In Architecture and others.
